blocks.content: The room told him what rooms always told him: the story without the explanation.

Man on the bed. Fully clothed. On top of the covers. Shoes by the door, neatly placed — not dropped, placed, with the deliberateness of someone who intended to do something after. Sleep an hour. Come back down.
The window. Open all the way, latch thrown back. Cold air moving through it freely, forty-seven degrees of it, for — Davenport checked the deputy's notes — at least seven hours.
Nightstand: a glass of water, roughly half full. A phone, face-down. A watch, removed and set parallel to the edge of the table — the habit of someone tidy, or anxious, or both.
Davenport stood in the center of the room and did not touch anything. He just looked.

Thirty years. He had stood in a lot of rooms. He knew what a room looked like when someone had gone to sleep and not woken up. He knew what a room looked like when something had happened and someone had tried to make it look like sleep.
This room looked like the first.

He looked at the window again. At the glass of water. At the way the man's arm lay at his side — not across his body, not tucked, just at his side, the flat natural rest of someone fully unconscious. Not the position of a man who had gotten cold and not closed the window.

blocks.speaker: Davenport
blocks.content: Get the coroner up here. I want eyes on this before anyone else touches anything.
